_all_text: Morganite morganite Be3Al2Si6O18 (with Mn2+/Mn3+) Manganese is the primary chromophore. Mn2+ gives pink; Mn3+ gives deeper pink to purplish. Cesium-rich Cs-beryl (pezzottaite) is related but is a distinct species (Cs-dominant). Irradiation produces orange tones; heat treatment removes these to give cleaner pink. Fe contamination produces brownish-orange overcast. Morganite's soft rose-peach color is inherently gentle and warm — less assertive than ruby, less cold than pink sapphire. Large clean crystals are available. The rose-gold + morganite combination became dominant in contemporary jewelry design in the 2010s. Morganite is a relatively recently named gem variety (1911) compared to emerald and aquamarine. George Frederick Kunz named it for J.P. Morgan, one of the great mineral collectors of the Gilded Age — whose collections are now at the American Museum of Natural History and Harvard's Mineralogical Museum. The 'morganite + rose gold' combination became a defining aesthetic of 2010s jewelry design, particularly in engagement rings. love and tenderness feminine warmth compassion rose-gold aesthetic gentle strength Named in 1911 by George Frederick Kunz (Tiffany & Co. head gemologist) for financier J.P. Morgan J.P. Morgan donated major mineral collections to AMNH and Harvard Gemstone use since naming; rose-gold combination popularized post-2010 morganite gemstone pink beryl peach beryl rose gold morganite J.P.
